one_line_takeaway: DAS shows that a two-stage dense-retrieval-plus-LLM-tournament pipeline can de-anonymize authors of anonymous peer reviews, blog posts, emails, and papers among tens of thousands of candidates at rates hundreds to a thousand times above random chance, exposing a concrete, LLM-enabled privacy threat to double-blind review and other anonymity-dependent systems.
key_finding: On the real ICLR review de-anonymization task, DAS reaches 28% Rank@5, 40% Rank@10, 44% Rank@15/20, with a 56% miss rate at k=20, versus an exact combinatorial random baseline of 0.03% Rank@5 and 0.13% Rank@20 (paper states this is roughly a 1,000-fold, and in the Appendix a precisely derived 338x, improvement over chance depending on the pool parameters used). On the Blog Authorship Corpus one-to-many test (Qwen1.5-72B-Chat), DAS reaches 95.0% Rank@1 / 100% Rank@5 (2-author) and 75.0% Rank@1 / 85.0% Rank@5 (5-author) versus AIDBench's 66.7%/93.3% and 66.7%/86.7% respectively; DAS also reports 75.0% Precision@5 in the 5-author blog setting. On Enron emails (same setup), DAS reaches 85.0% Rank@1 (2-author) and 80.0% Rank@1 (5-author) versus AIDBench's 66.7%/66.7%, with DAS reaching 77.0%/72.0% Precision@5 in the 2-/5-author settings vs AIDBench's 66.0%/49.3%. In-the-wild search over full corpora: on blogs DAS rises from 74% Rank@5 to 94% Rank@20 (6% miss); on Enron emails from 74% Rank@5 to 88% Rank@20 (12% miss). The progressive-elimination ablation on blogs shows DAS-PE causes a 34% relative Rank@5 drop and roughly doubles the miss rate, with Rank@20 stagnating at 68% vs. 94% for full DAS. Cross-model robustness on the Research Paper dataset (2-author, one-to-many): Claude-3.5-Sonnet reaches 100% Rank@1, DeepSeek-R1 reaches 96.3% Rank@1 (both also reported alongside the paper's original Gemini/Qwen results). On the Research Paper dataset overall, DAS gets 93.3% Rank@1 (2-author) and 60.0% Rank@1 (5-author) one-to-many, vs. AIDBench's 70.0% and 13.3%; in the real-scenario/in-the-wild paper-author task DAS reaches 92% Rank@20 with an 8% miss rate.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is De-Anonymization at Scale (DAS), a two-stage LLM-based authorship-attribution pipeline for open-set de-anonymization among tens of thousands of candidate texts with no pre-labeled author profiles. Stage 1 (Coarse Filtering) uses a CoSENT-based sentence-embedding model to compute cosine similarity between a target document and the corpus, shrinking the candidate pool (e.g. from up to 10^5 to a few thousand, top-2000 in the review experiments) while preserving high recall. Stage 2 (Tournament-Style Attribution, TSA) is a 'survival of the fittest' progressive-elimination tournament: candidates are randomly shuffled into groups of size l, an LLM ('LLMRank') is prompted to rank each group by stylistic similarity to the target and the top 2 of each group survive (top pick gets 2*phi points, runner-up gets phi points), the pool is iteratively re-grouped and re-scored until |O| <= k, and phi grows across rounds by a dynamic weighting factor phi_{tau+1} = alpha*phi_tau. The whole tournament is repeated for m independent trials with fresh random shuffles (majority-voting-style aggregation) and scores are summed across trials to produce the final ranked top-k list. An ablation variant, DAS-PE, disables progressive elimination (single round of grouping/scoring, no iterative reduction) to isolate that component's contribution.
threat_model: Adversary goal: link an anonymous document (e.g. a double-blind peer review, a blog post, an email, or a submitted paper) to its author by exploiting stylometric/writing-style signals, in an open-set setting with tens of thousands of candidates and no labeled per-author training profiles. Concretely demonstrated attack scenario: an attacker with access to a large pool of anonymized OpenReview reviews (or a reviewer's/author's prior public writing, e.g. their other papers or blog/email history) queries DAS with one anonymous target text and attempts to retrieve other texts by the same author, thereby compromising double-blind peer review, whistleblower forums, or other anonymous communication platforms. The paper frames this purely as an attack/risk-demonstration paper (with an explicit Ethical Considerations section on dual-use risk, data minimization for the human study, and responsible-disclosure-style withholding of 'operational instructions'); it proposes no formal system defense of its own, only forward-looking suggestions (stylometric audits, reviewer-style obfuscation tools, hybrid LLM+classical-stylometry defenses) as future work, not implemented or evaluated in the paper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: experiments focus mainly on Gemini-2.0-flash and Qwen1.5-72B-Chat and 'may not fully reflect the behavior of other strong LLM architectures'; prompt design is 'relatively limited,' potentially under-exploiting LLMs' fine-grained stylometric-analysis capacity. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own reported setup (not authors' explicit list but grounded in text): the ICLR real-scenario ground truth relies on a very small self-reported human study (9 participants, 25 test cases, one-shot self-judgment) rather than verified authorship labels; there is no comparison against a live human-adversary baseline (the paper explicitly substitutes an exact combinatorial random-guessing baseline because 'repeated human trials are infeasible'); and the paper implements/evaluates no defense or mitigation -- proposed countermeasures (stylometric audits, reviewer-style obfuscation, hybrid stylometric+LLM defenses) are mentioned only as future-work suggestions in the Conclusion, not built or tested.

datasets_used: ICLR 2023-2025 OpenReview reviews (23,889 submissions, 147,367 anonymized reviews, collected via OpenReview API)
Blog Authorship Corpus (Schler et al. 2006) - sampled 1,500 authors x 10 posts (also described in Appendix D as 15,000 posts by 1,500 bloggers)
Enron Email corpus (Klimt and Yang 2004) - 174 authors x 50 emails subset following Huang et al. (2024a) (Appendix D: 8,700 emails from 174 executives)

Research Paper dataset derived from AIDBench (Wen et al. 2024): 138,652 arXiv CS.LG papers (2019-2024) filtered to 24,095 papers from 1,500 authors with >=10 papers each (Appendix D reports 26,632 documents/1,500 authors in the summary table)
quantitative_results: ICLR reviews (real scenario): DAS 28%/40%/44%/44% Rank@5/10/15/20, 56% miss; Random baseline 0.03%/0.07%/0.10%/0.13% at the same cutoffs, 99.88% miss
Blog one-to-many (Qwen1.5-72B-Chat), 2-author: DAS Rank@1/3/5 = 95.0/100.0/100.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/93.3/93.3; DAS Prec@1/3/5 = 95.0/85.0/76.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/73.3/70.0
Blog one-to-many, 5-author: DAS Rank@1/3/5 = 75.0/85.0/85.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/86.7/86.7; DAS Prec@1/3/5 = 75.0/66.7/60.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/60.0/56.7
Enron one-to-many, 2-author: DAS Rank@1/3/5 = 85.0/100.0/100.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/93.3/100.0; DAS Prec@1/3/5 = 85.0/83.3/77.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/68.9/66.0
Enron one-to-many, 5-author: DAS Rank@1/3/5 = 80.0/95.0/100.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/73.3/80.0; DAS Prec@1/3/5 = 80.0/71.7/72.0 vs AIDBench 66.7/53.3/49.3
In-the-wild Enron emails: DAS Rank@5/10/15/20 = 74%/86%/88%/88%, 12% miss
Ablation (blog): DAS Rank@5/10/15/20/Miss = 74/88/92/94/6 vs DAS-PE 40/60/66/68/32 (34% relative Rank@5 drop, roughly doubled miss rate)
Model ablation, Research Paper dataset, 2-author one-to-many: DeepSeek-R1 Rank@1/3/5 = 96.3/100.0/100.0; Claude-3.5-Sonnet Rank@1/3/5 = 100.0/100.0/100.0
Model ablation, 5-author: DeepSeek-R1 Rank@1/3/5 = 85.2/88.9/96.3; Claude-3.5-Sonnet Rank@1/3/5 = 93.3/100.0/100.0

Research Paper dataset one-to-many, 2-author: DAS Rank@1 93.3% vs AIDBench 70.0% (23.3-pt gain); 5-author: DAS Rank@1 60.0% vs AIDBench 13.3%; DAS Prec@3 (5-author) 48.9% vs AIDBench 10.0% (38.9-pt gain)
Research Paper dataset real-scenario: DAS Rank@5/10/15/20 = 66%/80%/88%/92%, 8% miss

Appendix combinatorial derivation: with N=44,770, m=4, k=20 the exact random Rank@20 = 44,764C20/44,770C20 ~ 0.014%, and the paper states DAS's 44% Rank@20 on ICLR data is a ~338x improvement over this random chance figure
baselines_compared: Random guessing, computed via an exact hypergeometric/combinatorial formula (Rank@k(random) = 1 - C(N-m,k)/C(N,k)), used for the ICLR real-scenario and Research-Paper real-scenario experiments
AIDBench (Wen et al. 2024), an existing LLM-based authorship-identification benchmark/framework, used as the algorithmic baseline for the Blog, Enron, and Research Paper one-to-many experiments
DAS-PE (internal ablation: DAS with progressive elimination disabled, single-round grouping/scoring) used to isolate the contribution of the tournament's iterative elimination mechanism
